data.title: Czech Defense Company CSG to Build Slovak Artillery Propellant Plant Under JV
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo

Czech defense company CSG Group said it would build an artillery propellant plant in Slovakia after forming a joint venture with France's Eurenco.

CSG said Wednesday that ammunition company ZVS Holding, which it partly owns, will form a joint venture with Eurenco, a French manufacturer of explosives, propellant and military fuels.

The partnership aims to build an artillery propellant plant in the eastern Slovakian town of Strazske, with an investment of around 300 million euro ($348.4 million), the Amsterdam-listed company said. Construction of the plant should start in the near future, with production expected to kick off in 2028, CSG added.

The move comes as arms makers all over Europe take steps to increase production of ammunition amid greater governmental demand, as European countries seek to replenish their stocks.

However, European defense groups have been constrained by a shortage of propellant charge systems in their efforts to produce artillery ammunition.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: U.K. Finances Could Face Renewed Pressure — Market Talk
data.description: U.K. Treasury Chief Rachel Reeves's presentation before lawmakers this week revealed improvement in the fiscal headroom but the public finances still face significant challenges, UBS economists say in a note. The Middle East war highlights the need for higher defense spending, the economists say. Domestic political pressures could also resurface as the U.K. heads into local elections in May, they say. "Renewed political instability could lead to increased focus on the government's fiscal credibility amid a risk of easing of the fiscal rules." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: US natural gas futures fall over 4% as market stabilizes following prior gains
data.description:
U.S. natural gas futures fell more than 4% on Wednesday as the market stabilized after hitting an over one-week-high in the previous session, while investors assessed Middle East supply risks amid the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.

Front-month gas futures for April delivery on the New York Mercantile Exchange traded 10.7 cents lower, or 3.5%, to $2.95 per million British thermal units at 09:50 a.m. ET. The contract hit its highest since February 23 in the previous session.

The U.S.–Iran war widened after a U.S. strike hit an Iranian warship off ​Sri Lanka, deepening a crisis that has paralyzed shipping through the Strait of Hormuz for a fifth day and choked off ‌vital Middle East oil and gas flows.

"Given today's early price weakness, the most likely course of trade through the rest of this week could be some price consolidation between about the $2.90 level on the downside and yesterday's high ... on the upside," Ritterbusch & Associates said in a note.

"Market has largely shrugged off the huge price advances across the oil complex as well as European gas prices that have responded much more assertively to the US/Iran war than in the US gas market."

SUPPLY AND DEMAND

Average gas output in the Lower 48 states has held at 109.5 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) so far in March, according to data from financial firm LSEG. That reading compares with a monthly record high of 110.6 bcfd in December 2025.

U.S. energy firms likely pulled 55 billion cubic feet of natural gas from storage last week when warmer-than-normal weather kept heating demand low, according to the average estimate in a Reuters poll released on Wednesday.

LSEG projected average gas demand in the Lower 48 states, including exports, would drop from 123.3 bcfd this week to 112.7 bcfd next week.

Average gas flows to the nine large U.S. LNG export plants have eased to 18 bcfd so far in March, down from a record 18.7 bcfd in February.

QatarEnergy has declared force majeure on shipments of liquefied natural gas (LNG), it said in a statement on Wednesday, after attacks on its production facilities amid the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.

Dutch and British gas prices were also lower on Wednesday morning, after soaring earlier this week, but could remain volatile as the market tries to gauge how long Qatari supply of LNG will remain disrupted.

However, oil remained within sight of multi-month highs as the Middle East conflict disrupted energy flows, although prices pared gains after a report that Iranian operatives sought talks with the U.S. to end the conflict.

data.title: Canadian Small Businesses Question U.S. As a Trading Partner — Market Talk
data.description: About half of Canada's small businesses no longer consider the U.S. a reliable trading partner, data from the Canadian Federation of Independent Business shows. "Small businesses have faced massive uncertainty since the trade battle began last year," says CFIB President Dan Kelly, adding companies have been trying to keep up U.S. tariffs, including many that don't happen or are revised within hours. The study finds 52% of small business don't view the U.S. as a reliable trading partner, and three quarters say tariffs have strained relationships with U.S. partners or clients. With the Canada-U.S.-Mexico trade pact coming up for review in the months ahead, the stakes are even higher, Kelly says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Abercrombie Raises Prices to Offset Additional Tariffs — Market Talk
data.description: Abercrombie & Fitch's full-year outlook for operating margins of 12% to 12.5% assumes an incremental tariff impact of about $40 million, net of mitigation, CFO Robert Ball says on a call with analysts. "We feel good about the mitigation strategies that we put in place," he adds, noting the clothing company has made changes to its sourcing and negotiated deals with its suppliers. Abercrombie has also raised prices, beginning late in 4Q and continuing throughout the current quarter as more spring products launch. As a result, Ball says the company expects slight average unit retail improvements in 1Q, "and then that'll build throughout the balance of the year." (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Apple's $599 MacBook Neo: The Pros and Cons — WSJ
data.description:
By Nicole Nguyen

Apple unveiled a new MacBook Neo on Wednesday with a price tag of $599. That's historically low for the company, and comes days after it announced its latest "affordable" phone, the iPhone 17e, which also starts at $599. The Neo is available March 11, with preorders starting Wednesday.

The new MacBook is just over half the price of the M5 MacBook Air, also announced this week. Let's look at what you get — and don't get — with this new cheap laptop.

Pros

It has many of the features shoppers would look for in an Apple laptop: a sturdy aluminum case and two USB-C ports. Apple even says it gets up to 16 hours of battery life, a few hours shy of the MacBook Air.

If you upgrade to the $699 model, you can get Touch ID for secure access and Apple Pay.

It's just a hair thicker than the MacBook Air (and the same weight) and comes in four color options: silver, indigo, the pinkish "blush" and new lime-green "citrus" — and the keyboards are color-coordinated.

Cons

So, what's the catch? The biggest thing is that the MacBook Neo runs on a less powerful chip, the A18 Pro, a previous-generation processor that powered the iPhone 16 Pro. The chip supports Apple Intelligence AI features, but might fall short in some tasks.

Its 13-inch screen is a bit smaller and less featured than the Air's, and you can only attach a single external 4K monitor.

The FaceTime camera has a resolution of 1080p, similar to what's on older MacBook Airs but not as good as the newer 12-megapixel cameras with Center Stage that the top-selling Airs have now. It has two speakers, instead of the Air's four-speaker array.

And the keyboard doesn't have backlighting. Maybe that's something you don't care about when you're ordering, but come to find later that you miss it.

The $699 model not only adds Touch ID but also doubles the internal storage from 256GB to 512GB. However, you can't upgrade the RAM: It's fixed at 8GB.

Final thoughts

With the MacBook Neo, Apple is establishing a new baseline for its laptop line. A basic iPad-plus-keyboard also starts at $599. You can do a surprising amount of work on it, but not as much as on a Mac.

This is meant to square off against higher-end Chromebooks. Those education-focused laptops can cost up to $800 (though also as low as $100). And some Windows PC laptops are priced in a similar range.

Google made up nearly half of K-12 device purchases globally last year, while Apple's devices hovered around 20%, according to Futuresource Consulting's principal analyst Michael Boreham. (That includes laptops and tablets.) Perhaps the Neo — a MacBook priced like a Chromebook — could up Apple's popularity in the classroom.

My biggest question is whether an iPhone chip is powerful enough for desktop work. You should see how many browser tabs I keep open on my computer! I'll try the laptops later today, so stay tuned for my impressions.

Write to Nicole Nguyen at nicole.nguyen@wsj.com

data.title: SunOpta 2025 10-K: Revenue $817.7M, EPS $0.13
data.description:
SunOpta reported fiscal 2025 revenue of $817.7 million, a 13.0% increase year-over-year driven primarily by volume and mix gains, and delivered diluted earnings per share from continuing operations of $0.13. The company moved from a prior-year net loss to net earnings of $15.8 million, supported by higher gross profit and lower selling, general and administrative expenses. Operational improvements and capacity investments underpinned the performance while the company addressed tariff and sourcing challenges.

Financial Highlights

  • Revenues: $817.7 million, increase of 13.0% vs. prior year (volume/mix growth +13.5%, price -0.2%).
  • Gross profit: $115.9 million; gross margin 14.2% (adjusted gross margin 14.8%; reported 90 basis points year-over-year improvement).
  • Operating income: $39.7 million; operating margin 4.9% (increase of $24.1 million driven by higher gross profit and lower SG&A).
  • Net earnings (loss): $15.8 million, an improvement from the prior-year net loss of $(17.4) million.
  • Earnings per share — diluted: $0.13 from continuing operations for the year ended Jan 3, 2026.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue growth drivers: Growth in 2025 was driven by volume and mix gains across beverages, broths and fruit snacks, supporting expanded distribution.
  • Channel and brand momentum: Organic growth and new product launches expanded presence across retail, club, foodservice and e-commerce channels.
  • Operational efficiency: Improved plant utilization due to higher volumes, partially offset by manufacturing inefficiencies and ramp costs at the Midlothian facility.
  • Capacity and capital projects: Completed capital expansions that increased depreciation; management plans $35–40 million of capital expenditures and expects additional lease capacity in 2026.
  • Tariffs and sourcing: Implemented alternative sourcing and pricing to mitigate impacts from Canada/Mexico tariffs and is assessing effects of new worldwide tariffs.

Original SEC Filing: SunOpta Inc. [ STKL ] - 10-K - Mar. 04, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: USDA Reports New Corn Flash Sale — Market Talk
data.description: The USDA has announced a new flash sale of U.S. corn exports to unknown destinations, with 125,000 metric tons sold for delivery in the 2025/26 marketing year. It's the second consecutive day that the USDA has reported new corn sales to unknown destinations--often used as another designator for China, although not necessarily the case. Strength in the U.S. dollar makes American corn exports more expensive on the world stage. "Argentina remains slightly cheaper than the U.S. but U.S. exportable supplies obviously are significantly greater than Argentina's and U.S. export demand is expected to continue strong despite the sharp rally in the U.S. dollar this week," says the Hightower Report in a note. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Rheinmetall Buys Majority Stake in Croatian Military Vehicles Manufacturer DOK-ING
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo

Rheinmetall said it bought a majority stake in Croatian unmanned military vehicles manufacturer DOK-ING with the aim of expanding its drone portfolio.

The German defense group said Wednesday that it had acquired a 51% stake in DOK-ING, while its founder Vjekoslav Majetic will become the only other owner of the company, holding the remaining 49% stake.

The transaction will help Rheinmetall boost its portfolio of uncrewed and autonomous systems for military applications, and capitalize on DOK-ING's expertise on uncrewed solutions for mine clearance and operations in hazardous environments, the German company said.

Founded in 1991, DOK-ING will continue to be headquartered in Zagreb after this transaction, and preserve its management team, Rheinmetall said.

The combination of Rheinmetall's expertise in tactical vehicles and DOK-ING's capabilities in uncrewed systems will create a strong market position for Rheinmetall with considerable potential for the future, according to Bjorn Bernhard, chief of Rheinmetall's Vehicle Systems Europe division.

The move will also allow Rheinmetall to establish a foothold in Croatia, he said, describing the Balkan country as a "highly interesting customer."

Financial details for the transaction weren't disclosed.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: Milei appoints chief Buenos Aires prosecutor as new justice minister
data.description:
By Lucila Sigal

Argentine President Javier Milei said on Wednesday he has appointed chief Buenos Aires prosecutor Juan Bautista Mahiques as the country's new justice minister.

Mahiques, who has been the attorney general of the Argentine capital since October 2019, will replace Mariano Cuneo Libarona, who had been justice minister since December 2023, Milei announced in a posting on X.

Local newspaper La Nacion reported that Libarona had cited personal reasons for his departure.

"I assume this role with the conviction that without legal certainty there is no investment, without stable rules there is no development, and without independent judges there is no republic," Mahiques said in a separate post on X.

In a speech before Congress last Sunday, Milei announced that this year he will seek to pass more reforms to the penal code to introduce harsher sentences for criminals.

The government won several legislative victories last week when Congress approved a controversial labor reform and a change to the penal code that lowers the age of criminal responsibility from 16 to 14.

data.title: Canada Services PMI Remains in Contraction Even as It Climbs to 46.5 in February
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

Activity in Canada's services industry continued to weaken last month, with demand remaining weak and new orders falling, data released Wednesday showed.

The S&P Global Canada services purchasing managers index came in at 46.5 in February. That marked a fourth straight month below the 50 threshold between expansion and contraction, though it was a modest pickup after a slip to 45.8 in the first month of the year.

Services companies in the country continued to report a challenging operating environment, with clients showing uncertainty and a cautious attitude when committing to new business, the survey found.

"Canada's service-sector economy remained mired in a downturn during February, with both activity and new business falling again, albeit to weaker degrees," Paul Smith, economics director at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said.

The continued weakening in the services environment contrasts with manufacturing conditions in Canada, which have improved so far this year despite lingering concerns about trade and U.S. tariffs. S&P Global's purchasing managers index for the industry nudged up to 51 in February, marking a second straight month above the 50 mark following a year in contraction territory.

The Bank of Canada has projected soft economic growth ahead as supply chains are restructured and businesses adapt to the evolving global trade environment. Gross domestic product contracted 0.6% on an annualized basis in the final quarter of 2025, largely thanks to a big withdrawal from business inventories that offset growth in exports and increases in both household and government spending.

S&P Global's services survey showed the decline in new orders has now extended to 15 months, though the pace of the pullback in February was the softest since last October. Services providers reported a mixture of lower sales to both domestic and foreign clients, with new export business volumes declining again for the month.

A lack of new work meant companies on average either chose to not replace job leavers or were forced into layoffs during February, S&P Global said. Employment for the sector declined for a sixth successive month although, as with other variables measured by the survey, to a lesser degree than in January.

Even with reduced capacity, levels of work outstanding were reduced but companies continued to report that labor costs remained a key source of higher operating expenses in February. S&P Global said firms also noted a general increase in supplier charges, though input price inflation maintained its recent easing trend.

The survey showed that confidence in the outlook remained positive overall in February, with sentiment edging up to its highest level since October amid hopes that demand will pick up, though uncertainty persisted in the outlook, most notably in relation to trade and tariffs. Sentiment overall remained below trend, S&P Global said.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: U.S. economy gained strength February despite winter storm Fern. ISM survey hits 3 1/3 year high.
data.description:
By Jeffry Bartash

Businesses say they are adjusting to tariffs

Customers wait for food orders outside the Wienerschnitzel American hot dog and fast food restaurant in Los Angeles.

Check back for updates.

The largest part of the U.S. economy expanded in February at the fastest pace in 3 1/2 years, a survey showed, as the damage caused by high U.S. tariffs eased and sales and new orders rose.

A survey of service companies such as banks, retailers and restaurants was climbed to 56.1% in February from 53.8 in the prior month, the Institute for Supply Management said Wednesday.

Any number above 50% is a sign that business is growing. The index has topped this key threshold for 20 months in a row.

"The business climate remains solid overall," a top executive at a real estate company told ISM.

Market reaction: The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA and S&P 500 SPX rose in Wednesday trading.

-Jeffry Bartash

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Moderna's $2.25 Billion Patent Settlement Boosts Shares — WSJ
data.description:
By Peter Loftus

Moderna stock rose more than 6% Wednesday morning after the company agreed to pay up to $2.25 billion to two other biotech companies to settle patent litigation over its Covid-19 vaccine.

Despite the expense, analysts said the settlement avoids a worst-case scenario for Moderna of having to pay more to resolve the litigation. The settlement averts a trial that was scheduled to begin next week.

The settlement stems from a 2022 lawsuit filed in federal court in Delaware by two small biotech firms, Arbutus Biopharma and Genevant Sciences, alleging Moderna's Covid-19 shot infringed on several of their patents. The patents covered tiny substances called lipid nanoparticles that encase the genetic material in the vaccine. Moderna had claimed it used its own proprietary technology and didn't infringe on the patents.

Under the terms of the deal, Moderna will pay the other companies $950 million upfront, and then may owe an additional payment of $1.3 billion if Moderna loses an appeal of a prior court ruling related to the case.

Moderna will receive a license to the patented technology as part of the settlement. Arbutus and Genevant also are pursuing patent litigation against Pfizer and BioNTech for their Covid-19 vaccine, part of a wave of lawsuits over who invented Covid-19 shots.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).
